description:
GOAL: Lock the cross-process atom-table policy. Document the decision so
fz-ul4.19.3 (send/receive deep-copy) and downstream tickets can rely on
stable semantics. v1 SHIPS WITH A MUTEX FROM DAY ONE because the
libdispatch model (fz-ul4.11.32 + fz-ul4.19.1) means tasks may run on
different workers concurrently and intern() is write-shared.

## Decision (user, 2026-05-11): GLOBAL SHARED, MUTEX-PROTECTED v1

The `fz_runtime` crate's atom table remains a process-global singleton
across all fz-level tasks. All tasks share atom ids. The intern table
is mutex-protected; lookups (`name_of`) and intern are both serialized.

Architectural reasoning (per .19 epic): "ONE shared address space
across all processes — NOT BEAM-style separate spaces". Atoms behave
like shared frozen literals.

## Why mutex, not "no sync" (v1)

- fz-ul4.19.1 ships a worker pool (size 1 default, configurable). Even
  at pool size 1 we want the abstractions correct so pool size > 1
  works without code changes.
- intern() writes; name_of() reads. Both must be coherent across
  workers. Plain HashMap is not Send + Sync. Mutex<HashMap> is.
- Performance: intern is rare (atoms are usually compile-time literals
  baked into code; runtime intern is exceptional). name_of is read-only
  but with a Mutex incurs a lock per call. Acceptable for v1; revisit
  if profile shows contention.

## Why we did NOT pick per-process

- Forces atom-id translation on every send (.19.3).
- Breaks .19.5's zero-copy arena transfer: atom ids in a transferred
  arena would be wrong in the receiver's table.
- Clean isolation has no proven value — atoms are part of the program's
  static interface.

## Why we did NOT pick tiered shared+private

- Two-tier complexity (frozen shared region + per-process append) buys
  isolation we don't need. Reserved if intern() becomes contended.

## Performance follow-up (NOT this ticket)

When measurement shows mutex contention on intern() / name_of() under
multi-worker load:

- Option A: RwLock<HashMap>. name_of becomes uncontended (read side
  parallel); intern still serialized.
- Option B: lock-free hash (DashMap, flurry, hand-rolled CAS-on-bucket).
- Option C: per-worker name_of cache that hits the shared table only
  on miss.

User decision (2026-05-11): SINGLE MUTEX for v1. Don't optimize until
contention is measured.

## Deliverables

This is a DECISION RECORD + minimal code:

- Audit `fz_runtime::intern` / `name_of` (called from src/jit.rs:230,
  264 — note jit.rs itself is `#![cfg(any())]` gated, so those call
  sites are dead; verify the actual call sites in active code).
- Wrap the intern HashMap in a Mutex<HashMap> (if not already). The
  crate is at runtime/; this ticket either updates it OR confirms the
  current state is already mutex-protected.
- Add a one-line comment near each active call site:
  `// Atom table is process-global, mutex-protected; see fz-ul4.19.6.`

## Tests

- A test that two tasks (via .19.1 Runtime with pool size 2 if feasible,
  or sequential tasks on pool size 1) intern `:ok` independently; the
  two ids are equal. Confirms shared semantics.
- A test that an atom sent A -> B via send (post-.19.3) preserves
  identity: receiver compares received `:ok` to its own literal `:ok`
  and they're equal.

## Out of scope

- Implementing send (.19.3 owns that).
- RwLock / lock-free atom table (follow-up if contention measured).
- Atom GC (atoms are pinned for program lifetime, matching BEAM v1).

## References

- runtime/ crate (`fz_runtime::intern`, `name_of`)
- fz-ul4.19 epic body — "ONE shared address space"
- fz-ul4.19.3 — consumes this decision in deep-copy walker
- fz-ul4.19.5 — relies on shared atom ids for arena transfer

## Acceptance

- Atom table is mutex-protected (verify, change if not).
- Decision recorded as a comment at active intern / name_of call sites.
- Two confirmation tests added.
- One commit referencing fz-ul4.19.6.
title: Atom-table cross-process policy: global shared singleton (v1)
